title: Retrieval Quality: Dense, Sparse, and Hybrid: Check your understanding

questions.question: A user queries your technical support RAG system with 'ERR-4021 authentication failure'. Your dense-only retrieval returns general articles about authentication errors but misses the specific ERR-4021 troubleshooting page. What is the most likely cause?
options: The embedding model has too few dimensions to capture specific error codes
Dense retrieval finds semantic similarity but may miss exact term matches: 'ERR-4021' has no semantic meaning the model can generalise from, so sparse (keyword) retrieval is needed to catch it
The error code document was not included in the index
The query is too short for dense retrieval to work effectively
questions.explanation: Dense retrieval searches by meaning, not by exact words. 'ERR-4021' is an opaque identifier: it has no semantic relationship to other error codes, so an embedding model produces a poor representation for it. BM25 (sparse retrieval) catches it through exact term matching: the term 'ERR-4021' in the query scores directly against the same term in documents using term frequency and IDF, with no dependence on training data. Hybrid search combines both: the exact match from BM25 and semantic matches from dense retrieval.

questions.question: You want to combine dense and sparse retrieval scores into a single ranked list. Why is Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) preferred over directly summing the two scores?
options: RRF is faster to compute than score addition
Dense scores (cosine similarity, 0–1) and sparse scores (BM25, unbounded) are not on the same scale; RRF operates on ranks instead, requiring no normalisation
RRF gives more weight to sparse results, which are generally more accurate
Score addition requires both retrieval systems to return the same number of results
questions.explanation: BM25 scores are unbounded (a document can score 14.3 or 0.7 depending on corpus statistics) while cosine similarity is bounded between -1 and 1. Directly summing them gives BM25 an arbitrary weight advantage. RRF sidesteps this entirely by using only the rank position, not the score: the contribution of each result is 1/(k + rank), which is comparable across any retrieval method regardless of score scale.

questions.question: You add a cross-encoder re-ranker to your pipeline and retrieve top-5 results before re-ranking. Users still get poor answers. What is the most likely problem?
options: Cross-encoders are less accurate than bi-encoders for re-ranking tasks
Re-ranking can only reorder the candidates it receives: if the correct document is not in the top-5 before re-ranking, no re-ranker can surface it
The cross-encoder model needs to be fine-tuned on your domain to work correctly
Re-ranking only works when dense and sparse retrieval are both used
questions.explanation: A re-ranker cannot surface documents it never sees. If the correct document ranks 8th after initial retrieval and you only pass the top-5 to the re-ranker, the correct document is excluded before re-ranking even begins. The fix is to widen the initial retrieval window (retrieve top-20 or top-50) before passing candidates to the re-ranker. This is why the pattern is: retrieve wide, re-rank narrow.

questions.question: Which retrieval metric most directly answers the question 'does my system find the right document within the results I pass to the model?'
options: BLEU score: measures overlap between the retrieved text and the expected answer
Recall@k: the fraction of queries where the correct document appears in the top-k retrieved results
Latency p95: measures whether retrieval is fast enough for production
Cosine similarity score: the average similarity between query and retrieved documents
questions.explanation: Recall@k directly answers the retrieval question: for k results passed to the model, what fraction of the time does the correct document appear in those k results? If recall@5 is 0.60, 40% of the time the model never sees the right document: no amount of prompt engineering can fix that. BLEU measures generation quality, latency measures speed, and average cosine similarity is a weak proxy that doesn't tell you whether the right document was retrieved.

questions.question: Your RAG system's recall@20 is 0.91 but recall@5 is 0.58. The correct document is often retrieved but rarely in the top 5. What should you add to your pipeline?
options: Switch to a higher-quality embedding model to improve the ranking of the correct document
Increase top-k to 20 and pass all 20 chunks to the model
Add a re-ranker: retrieve top-20 candidates (good recall) then re-score them to surface the correct document into the top-5 (better precision)
The problem is in the chunking strategy: the correct chunk is too large to rank highly
questions.explanation: High recall@20 with low recall@5 is the textbook case for a re-ranker. The right document is being retrieved, it's in the top-20 91% of the time, but the ranking order is wrong. A cross-encoder re-ranker, which scores (query, document) pairs jointly, is significantly more accurate at ordering than the bi-encoder used for initial retrieval. Passing all 20 chunks to the model wastes context window and dilutes the signal; switching embedding models wouldn't fix an ordering problem.
